content.text: Chunk Size & Overlap
Every RAG pipeline must answer two questions before it can split a document: how many tokens should each chunk hold, and how many tokens should consecutive chunks share? These two numbers —
chunk_size
and
chunk_overlap
— control the precision-vs-context trade-off more directly than almost any other pipeline setting, yet they are often left at an arbitrary default.

attrs.alt: Chunk size spectrum (64–2048 tokens) showing the sweet spot at 256–512 tokens, overlap mechanics with shared boundary tokens, and a quick-reference table of recommended settings by content type.

content.text: The diagram above captures the key insight: smaller chunks let the embedder focus on a single idea and let the retriever score precisely, but they strip away the surrounding sentences that might resolve an ambiguous pronoun or establish a necessary term. Larger chunks do the opposite.
What Chunk Size Controls
Embedding granularity.

An embedding model collapses the entire chunk into one vector. If a chunk contains three unrelated topics, the resulting vector is a blend of all three — none of them strongly. A 128-token chunk about a single concept embeds more faithfully than a 1 024-token section covering an introduction, three data points, and a conclusion.
ANN search precision.

When a query vector is compared against chunk vectors, a tightly focused chunk is more likely to surface exactly when its topic is queried and less likely to surface spuriously. Diluted chunks produce retrieval recall at the cost of precision.
LLM prompt footprint.

Every retrieved chunk occupies context-window space. If you retrieve k = 5 chunks and each chunk is 1 024 tokens, you spend 5 120 tokens before the LLM has seen the system prompt or the user query. With 256-token chunks the same k consumes 1 280 tokens, leaving far more room for the conversation.
What Overlap Controls

Overlap is the number of tokens at the end of chunk N that are repeated at the start of chunk N+1. Its sole purpose is continuity: if the splitting boundary lands inside a sentence or between two related clauses, the overlap carries enough context across the boundary to keep both chunks independently coherent.
Rule of thumb: set overlap to 10–15 % of chunk_size. For chunk_size = 512 that is 51–77 tokens; in practice 64 is a safe, round target.

Setting overlap above 25 % creates diminishing returns and measurable costs. Each duplicated token appears in multiple chunks, so the same text will score highly for many queries — introducing noise, inflating the index, and potentially surfacing the same passage twice in the context window.
Counting Tokens, Not Characters
Characters are an unreliable unit. A single Chinese character is one character but one token. An English word like
unambiguously
is still only 1–2 tokens; a code snippet full of underscores and curly braces tokenises very differently from prose. Use the same tokeniser your embedding model uses.
For open-source embedding models (e.g.
BAAI/bge-m3
), use the HuggingFace tokeniser directly:
Pass the
length_function
argument to LangChain splitters so they respect token counts rather than character counts:

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("text-embedding-3-small")

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=64,
    length_function=lambda text: len(enc.encode(text)),
    separators=["

", "
", ". ", " ", ""],
)
Setting Parameters in LangChain
The two primary arguments map directly to the concepts above:

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

# Dense prose: 512 tokens, 64 overlap (~12%)
prose_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=64,
)

# Q&A / fact sheets: 128 tokens, 16 overlap
qa_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=128,
    chunk_overlap=16,
)

docs = prose_splitter.split_documents(raw_docs)
print(f"{len(docs)} chunks from {len(raw_docs)} docs")
For hierarchical retrieval, create two splitters and use
ParentDocumentRetriever

from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

parent_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=1024, chunk_overlap=128)
child_splitter  = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=128,  chunk_overlap=0)

vectorstore = Chroma(embedding_function=OpenAIEmbeddings())
docstore    = InMemoryStore()

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=docstore,
    child_splitter=child_splitter,
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter,
)
retriever.add_documents(raw_docs)

# ANN search finds the small child; retriever returns the large parent
results = retriever.invoke("Q3 2025 revenue growth")
Finding the Right Size: Empirical Tuning
No formula yields the perfect chunk size without measuring. The recommended process is:
Build a golden evaluation set.
20–50 representative questions with known correct passages.
Index the same corpus at three sizes:
128, 256, 512 (and 1024 for long-document use cases).
Score retrieval with RAGAS or a custom hit-rate script.
Measure context_recall (did the correct passage appear in top k?) and context_precision (how much of the retrieved text was actually relevant?).
Pick the size with the best F1 trade-off.
Then set overlap ≈ 12% of that size.

from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import context_recall, context_precision
from datasets import Dataset

# Build your evaluation dataset
data = {
    "question":  ["What was Q3 2025 revenue?", ...],
    "contexts":  [retrieved_chunks_per_question, ...],   # list of lists of str
    "ground_truth": ["$2.1B", ...],
}
ds = Dataset.from_dict(data)

result = evaluate(ds, metrics=[context_recall, context_precision])
print(result)   # {'context_recall': 0.87, 'context_precision': 0.72}
Interaction with the Embedding Model's Max Length

Every embedding model has a hard token limit. Exceeding it silently truncates the input — the excess tokens are dropped before the embedding is computed, making long chunks less accurate than you expect.
Model
Token limit
Safe chunk_size
text-embedding-3-small
≤ 512 (precision focus)
text-embedding-3-large
BAAI/bge-m3
sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2
≤ 200 (critical!)
voyage-3
up to 2 048
The
all-MiniLM-L6-v2

model's 256-token limit is a frequent pitfall. With the default chunk_size of 1 000 characters (~250 tokens), chunks routinely exceed the model's max length and get silently truncated to the 256-token window, losing the last third of the chunk's text. Always verify against the model's documentation.
Diagnosing Bad Chunk Size
Signs that chunk size is misconfigured:
Retrieval finds the right document but misses the exact sentence:
chunk_size is too large; a smaller size isolates the relevant passage better.
Retrieval returns nothing or returns off-topic chunks:
chunk_size may be too small, splitting a multi-sentence definition across boundaries. Increase size or add overlap.
The LLM produces vague answers citing long passages:
chunks are too large; the LLM buries the answer inside filler.
Index size is much larger than expected:
overlap is too high (check for > 25%). Reduce or set to 10–15%.
The same passage appears twice in the context window:
overlap is duplicating tokens across highly ranked chunks. Lower overlap or deduplicate retrieved chunks by content hash before passing to the LLM.
Dynamic Chunk Sizing by Content Type

When a corpus mixes document types — emails, reports, code files, and transcripts — a single chunk_size fits none of them well. A practical pattern is to detect the content type during ingestion and route to a type-specific splitter:
The language-aware splitter (
from_language
) uses the syntax tree delimiters for that language — for Python it prefers
\nclass
\ndef
, and
as split points, keeping functions whole wherever possible.
Controlling Overlap at the Token Level
LangChain's
chunk_overlap
argument is specified in the same unit as
length_function
— characters by default, tokens when you pass a token counter. Always keep the two consistent:

import tiktoken
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("text-embedding-3-small")
tok = lambda text: len(enc.encode(text))

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=64,           # 64 tokens, ~12%
    length_function=tok,
    add_start_index=True,       # metadata: start_index tells you where in the doc
)

text = open("annual_report.txt").read()
chunks = splitter.create_documents([text])

# Inspect overlap
for i, c in enumerate(chunks[:3]):
    print(f"Chunk {i}: {tok(c.page_content)} tokens | start={c.metadata['start_index']}")
# Chunk 0: 508 tokens | start=0
# Chunk 1: 511 tokens | start=2227    ← 64-token window included from chunk 0
# Chunk 2: 504 tokens | start=4454
add_start_index=True
injects a
start_index
field into each chunk's metadata. This is invaluable for debugging: compute
start_index
len(page_content)
from two consecutive chunks and compare them — the difference should equal
chunk_size - chunk_overlap
characters (in character mode) or the equivalent in bytes. A mismatch indicates the splitter found a separator that forced a shorter chunk.
Overlap and Deduplication at Query Time

Overlap introduces a subtle retrieval artifact: two adjacent chunks share tokens, so they embed to nearby vectors. When a query falls squarely on that shared region, both chunks may appear in the top-k results, consuming context budget with essentially the same text.
A simple deduplication pass before passing chunks to the LLM removes near-duplicates:

import hashlib
from langchain_core.documents import Document

def dedup_chunks(docs: list[Document], *, keep: int = 5) -> list[Document]:
    """Remove chunks whose first 200 chars appeared in an already-selected chunk."""
    seen: set[str] = set()
    out:  list[Document] = []
    for doc in docs:
        sig = hashlib.md5(doc.page_content[:200].encode()).hexdigest()
        if sig not in seen:
            seen.add(sig)
            out.append(doc)
        if len(out) == keep:
            break
    return out

# Usage inside a retrieval chain
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 10})
dedup      = RunnableLambda(lambda docs: dedup_chunks(docs, keep=5))

chain = retriever | dedup
results = chain.invoke("Q3 2025 revenue by segment")
print(f"{len(results)} unique chunks in context")

The signature covers only the first 200 characters: enough to detect near-identical starts (which is the overlap pattern) without falsely deduplicating two chunks that happen to share a common introductory sentence.
Chunk Size and the Cost of Re-indexing

Changing chunk_size after initial indexing requires a full re-embed of the corpus. This is a deliberate decision, not a quick tweak, so it pays to benchmark before committing to production. The approximate re-indexing cost formula:
where
is the total token count across all documents and
is the price per million tokens for your embedding model. For
text-embedding-3-small
at $0.02 / M tokens, re-indexing a 100 M-token corpus costs $2. For
text-embedding-3-large
at $0.13 / M tokens the same job costs $13.

The incremental cost of re-indexing one document is proportional only to that document's token count, so you can safely experiment on a subset (e.g., one day's ingestion) before committing to a corpus-wide re-index.
Putting It Together: A Configurable Ingestion Pipeline

from dataclasses import dataclass
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
import tiktoken

@dataclass
class ChunkConfig:
    chunk_size:    int   = 512
    chunk_overlap: int   = 64
    model:         str   = "text-embedding-3-small"

    @property
    def length_function(self):
        enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(self.model)
        return lambda text: len(enc.encode(text))

def build_index(docs, cfg: ChunkConfig = ChunkConfig()):
    splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
        chunk_size=cfg.chunk_size,
        chunk_overlap=cfg.chunk_overlap,
        length_function=cfg.length_function,
        add_start_index=True,
    )
    chunks = splitter.split_documents(docs)

    embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model=cfg.model)
    vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(chunks, embeddings)

    print(
        f"Indexed {len(chunks)} chunks "
        f"(size={cfg.chunk_size}, overlap={cfg.chunk_overlap})"
    )
    return vectorstore

# Experiment: compare two configs on a RAGAS hit-rate benchmark
vs_small = build_index(raw_docs, ChunkConfig(chunk_size=256, chunk_overlap=32))
vs_large = build_index(raw_docs, ChunkConfig(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64))
Common Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Leaving chunk_size at LangChain's default (1 000 characters)
Inconsistent token counts; silently truncates on short-context models
Set explicitly in tokens, not characters; use length_function
Using the same chunk_size for all document types
Suboptimal for every type
Configure per document type or use content-adaptive splitting
Setting overlap = 0 on small chunks
Multi-sentence definitions and numbered lists are severed
Add ≥10% overlap or use sentence-boundary splitter
Setting overlap ≥ chunk_size / 2
Index bloat, duplicate retrieval hits, wasted embedding budget
Cap overlap at 15–20% of chunk_size
Never measuring retrieval quality
Default settings may be far from optimal for the data
Run a hit-rate or RAGAS eval over a small golden set
